content:
@Fiona Behrens
CC: @Alice Ryhl

In response to the PIO topic you brought up during today's meeting.

I think that on the Rust side we shouldn't use iowrite*/ioread*. The reason is that those functions always do the if-else dance in IO_COND(), which in Rust isn't necessary, since we can do this check once on creation of the Io type.

We can introduce a trait IoBackend with generic read / write functions, e.g. read8 or write32 and then implement an MMIO and a PIO type implementing those functions with the corresponding MMIO and PIO accessors. The generic Io type can then instantiate either one or the other on creation avoiding the duplicate checks we'd get with iowrite*/ioread*.

Hmm, how would we do that check on functions that just give us a cockie. For platforms that uses the iowrite from lib I do like that idea, but according to the docs there could be a arch that handles it differently
E.g. the pci_iomap function is documented to just return this cookie without reasonable way to check if pio or mmio
Will definitely try to code that down

If you look at pci_iomap(), it either returns the result of ioremap() which is a real memory mapping or the result of ioport_map(), which indeed is some kind of cookie. But in this case cookie means that the port is embedded in a "fake" address.

When you call e.g. ioread32() [1] the "fake" address is converted to the port again [2]. We should be able to do the same thing once on creation of Io, rather than on every read / write.

[1] https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v6.13.1/source/lib/iomap.c#L225
[2] https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v6.13.1/source/lib/iomap.c#L57
As you say, this is at least true for CONFIG_GENERIC_IOMAP=y. It might indeed be that otherwise we're screwed. :/
So, for CONFIG_GENERIC_IOMAP=n we probably need a backend for iowrite*/ioread*.
Maybe we translate iowrite* to Rust and let the IO_COND() check happen in Rust?
Instead of using a helper for iowrite*.

Alice Ryhl said:

Maybe we translate iowrite* to Rust and let the IO_COND() check happen in Rust?

But still storing the cookie in rust? Not sure how much this would help us
I mean, if you can do something better than what C does, that's even better of course.
You mean for still one composite type that is able to handle both. Hmm have to look at that
It is at least much easier to just do that as an enum or something like that as we can easier return types bigger than a pointer in rust
If C does it that way, then clearly the performance of it is acceptable?
Which way do you mean now? Think that could also be somewhat included in being able to just return a new type
I mean that C does a runtime check?
Ah, yeah could be fine
If you always know at compile time which variant it's going to be, then it's fine to get rid of the check, of course.
Still wonder if we could do it in a nice way with const generics. But as enums are AFAIK still not allowed, unsure how to reasonable implement a three state
I think if you want generics just use normal types with a trait instead of trying to use const generics.
Hehe yeah sounds reasonable
But one thing is, presumably C does the runtime check because you don't know at compile time which one you need? If so, then generics won't help you since they require you to know it at compile time?
Sometimes you know, sometimes not
I guess you can have three types, where one does the runtime check.
We could in the end then also use Box<dyn trait> but don’t like that to much
Nah, now you will have worse performance than c

Alice Ryhl said:

I guess you can have three types, where one does the runtime check.

Yep that was my idea now. That’s why three states

From what I see right now, u64 accessors are only defined for mmio, not pio and sometimes on iowrite, but also just the extern void but then mission implementations. Should I just not provide 64bit functions in the trait then?

very very rough sketch, but maybe like this could work. Just have to implement MMIO and the generic coockie base system now (and fix documentation and everything :) )
https://cyberchaos.dev/kloenk/linux/-/commit/c76d20c5f4bc0c4ec49042899e4ef33500c3a55e
You can have two traits, one with the 64-bit functions and one with the others. Then only implement the 64-bit trait when they're actually available.
yeah, would work as well
but we also have some of the relaxed and similar things as well, other option would be to just create some of those as "normal" functions
I think for PIO, e.g. read8_relaxed() can just call read8().

Danilo Krummrich said:

I think for PIO, e.g. read8_relaxed() can just call read8().

so you would like to have those in the trait as well?
I think they have to be, no?
We can also just add it to only the MMIO struct that implements the trait. Less fun to use, but think the coockie interface also does not have the relaxed e.g.
But how does that help? In Io we can only use the trait functions, can't we?
hmm yeah, but we can maybe generate a MMIO struct from IO
_relaxed btw. only means that they don't have a memory barrier.
But then it's not transparent to the user, I'd like to avoid that.
I think we don't need to worry about it too much, having PIO implement e.g. read8_relaxed() as read8() should be fine.
ok, so just both, can add that to my POC above later

I did another grep in my linux checkout. The only occurrences that are related to all this are directly on MMIO, there is nothing on the iowrite family and also not on pio, so it's fine to just do the relaxed ones on MMIO
Sure, but what do you suggest should Io::write8_relaxed() do if it's PIO?
I don't want to implement it at all
As only MMIO implements it, and not even the branching function, we can just implement it on that struct that is MMIO
But we have a common Io interface. Otherwise drivers have to know on compile time whether they are on PIO or MMIO.
But on the C side the relaxed version is also not on the common one, so there the driver has to know at compile time as well
force pushed my code, still don't have the relaxed, have to change the macro for that, to make generating it easier, hopefully monday evening or tuesday I will fix that

True, but what type would you return from pci::Device::iomap_region()?

In C you can use whatever accessor you want because the driver typically knows if a certain BAR is MMIO or PIO.
My idea was to make the generic version a enum. Then we can just match the enum and if it’s mmio return the mmio abstraction
So what exactly would be the return type of pci::Device::iomap_region()?
Probably this enum. Have to looks again
The enum implementation is also only for Generic iomap

https://cyberchaos.dev/kloenk/linux/-/commit/cb4245aaa9904283368f85ce83412bb724299162 this now implements the relaxed functions for MMIo, will work on how to create this enum now, so that the pci::Device::iomap_region() can return it

One thing I noticed right now, is that currently we deref pci::Bar to Io. With Io being the enum for cached access (we have to do the check at some point either way) this does not work. We could implement the new IoAccess traits for Bar directly now though. Only thing is how to attach a lifetime to the io enum when you want raw access
but we also have to transform the IoRaw for how I created the PIo function
also with proper handing Io::from_raw can fail, if the cookie is invalid. That should never happen, but guess with rust abstraction should is not good enough :)

https://cyberchaos.dev/kloenk/linux/-/commit/ccdf58166fdd8892b3eb542f0fb53ec07b57adc9 and a new version that implements everything based on CONFIG_GENERIC_IOMAP. have to see how to work without it (don't have a arm setup currently, so will be annoying to do, but will try)

On my end this doesn't compile with PCI drivers.

Maybe you can adjust samples/rust/rust_driver_pci.rs to show how it's supposed to work?
Yeah, also planned. Know that I did not yet get to that. The general api is more what the current commit tries to show

One thought that I forgot to mention: It may be good to send an early commit that changes the current function names of Io from readb() etc. to read8() etc. This may prevent some trouble once the actual thing you're working on is about to land.

(Actually, I already thought of the Io abstraction advancing when I worked on it initially and I also therefore wanted to rename things to more generic names, but I entirely forgot about it. :sad: )
You mean as very early commit just alone renaming it. Yeah could make sense

Fiona Behrens said:

You mean as very early commit just alone renaming it. Yeah could make sense

Or should I in that commit introduce all the macros and trait I have already, so we have the trait system and then just add the new things
also looked again at arm (not usually that much down at assembler level), that does not have portio, not sure how to best then change that Io is equals to MMIo
https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20250217-io-generic-rename-v1-1-06d97a9e3179@kloenk.dev/T/

Danilo Krummrich said:

On my end this doesn't compile with PCI drivers.

Maybe you can adjust samples/rust/rust_driver_pci.rs to show how it's supposed to work?

I'm not sure the "relaxed" accessors are supposed to be used with PCI. They were primarily added for non-PCI use on weakly ordered systems (Arm). In C we can't really enforce the usage, but with Rust we could I think. There's a lot of history around this. It would be good to get Arnd Bergmann's attention on it.

@Rob Herring

I don't really see how this is related to PCI? The relaxed semantics is about instruction ordering at compile time and CPU ordering at runtime. As you mentioned for ARM / ARM64 both are relevant, for x86 the relaxed semantics only omits the compiler barrier as in the non-relaxed version.

The PCI bus itself has strictly ordered reads (i.e., a read cannot pass a previous write), but may have unordered writes, e.g. posted writes (buffering in root complexes or switches) or write combining. But this is unrelated to the ordering of the CPU or the compiler.

So, on x86 it's probably not that interesting to be able to omit the compiler barrier. But on ARM / ARM64, PCI devices are typically mapped through a PCI root complex, i.e. PCI BAR accesses happens through accesses to the root complex and there we might care about unnecessary CPU barriers, no?

@Danilo Krummrich
The issue is drivers get developed on x86 which is SO both for PCI and the CPU. Arm has to ensure accesses behave the same or there may be problems with using those PCI devices on Arm. You can't have a WO CPU reordering things before they even get to the PCI bus. You would be back to Arm and x86 behaving differently if you used relaxed accessors on a PCI device. There's very few PCI drivers using relaxed accessors:

git grep --name-only 'struct pci_driver' drivers/ | xargs git grep readl_relaxed

Mostly all HiSilicon drivers, so maybe a questionable pattern... Given so few, do we even need/want relaxed accessors for PCI?

Also, I think the 'asm volatile' still serves as some compiler barrier as it is still necessary for register accesses to occur in program order. Otherwise, things could be really broken. But I'm a bit rusty on the ins and outs of asm volatile.

Again, just make sure the folks that have studied this are in the loop. And Arnd has the most cross-arch experience there.

You can't have a WO CPU reordering things before they even get to the PCI bus.

I think you can, but I agree that it very rarely is possible and even less often needed. Maybe some bulk reads / writes, where the order doesn't matter. (We may have a use-case in Nova where we could do it, but if we need it is a whole different question -- I think not.)

For the decision of whether to expose a separate type just to hide the relaxed functions, I think it should be fundamentally wrong to use them, but I don't think this is the case.

Anyway, I think this is a discussion that should be continued on the mailing list. I think we should keep a common interface for the first patch series and then discuss where we want to go from there.

git grep --name-only 'struct pci_driver' drivers/ | xargs git grep readl_relaxed

That only catches the ones that have the pci driver code and the relevant I/O stuff in the same file. But I fully agree, it's very rarely used.

Also, I think the 'asm volatile' still serves as some compiler barrier as it is still necessary for register accesses to occur in program order.

I don't think so, see also [1]. But even if, not all I/O accessor are implemented using asm volatile.

[1] https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Extended-Asm.html#Volatile
Arnd says the requirement for relaxed usage with PCI is it must be used carefully and documented.
what does that say for my patch when I finaly get to test the arm stuff and make it sendable? document in the relaxed trait that you likely don't want relaxed, but still offer it?

Fiona Behrens said:

what does that say for my patch when I finaly get to test the arm stuff and make it sendable? document in the relaxed trait that you likely don't want relaxed, but still offer it?

It's the callers that need to document their usage much like is done for unsafe blocks in rust. So you would just want to document the requirement to document usage. It would be nice if that could be checked by tools though.
we can make the functions in the trait unsafe, so that clippy checks there is a SAFETY comment, but that feels kinda wrong? not sure
A similar mechanism would be great, but yeah, making them unsafe doesn't seem right.

@Fiona Behrens

There have been a few discussions about the ergonomic aspects of the aspect that any Io implementations can only handed out by subsystems within a Devres container. For instance, PCI returns a Devres<pci::Bar> on iomap_region()`.

Correspondingly, I think we want additional accessors that take a closure and call try_access() internally and pass the I/O implementation (e.g. pci::Bar) to the closure, such that the corresponding RCU read-side critical section is self contained.

Would you be interested in looking into this as well?
Nt sure I fully understand what you mean. Also think my usecase is quite different currently. Right now I only need to access portio for my stuff
